pattern: Whether a house can be robbed right after its neighbor now depends on color, not just distance, so the running DP state has to remember not only the best total so far but also whether the previous house was actually taken and, if it was, which color it happened to be.
hints: This is a variant of the classic non-adjacent-houses problem, except the adjacency restriction only kicks in when two neighboring houses share the same color code -- neighbors with different colors may both be robbed.

Track two running totals while scanning left to right: the best total assuming the current house IS robbed, and the best total assuming it is NOT -- because whether the next house may be robbed depends on which of these happened here and what color this house was.

When robbing the current house, add its money to the better of the two previous totals -- but if the previous house shares this house's color, you may only add onto the total where the previous house was skipped, never onto the one where it was robbed.

edge_inputs.description: statement-style example: two non-adjacent houses give the best total
statement-style example: a mix of adjacent-different-color and non-adjacent houses
statement-style example: skipping one house because of a same-color neighbor
a single house is always worth robbing
two houses share a color, so only the larger one may be robbed
two houses have different colors, so both may be robbed
every house shares one color, reducing to the classic no-two-adjacent-houses problem
every house has a color different from its neighbor, so every house may be robbed
three houses at the maximum value with alternating colors, so every house can be robbed

stress.description: large-scale fully random houses and colors, exercising the linear scan at the size limit
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 100000) for _ in range(n)]
    colors = [rng.randint(1, 100000) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums, colors]

stress.description: large-scale houses with only a few distinct color codes, maximizing how often the same-color adjacency restriction actually applies
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 100000) for _ in range(n)]
    colors = [rng.randint(1, 3) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums, colors]

oracle_python:
import sys
from functools import lru_cache

class Solution:
    def rob(self, nums: List[int], colors: List[int]) -> int:
        # Independent top-down formulation: explicit memoized recursion keyed
        # on (index, whether the previous house was taken, its color), rather
        # than the optimal solution's rolling forward dp_take/dp_skip pair.
        n = len(nums)
        old_limit = sys.getrecursionlimit()
        sys.setrecursionlimit(max(old_limit, n + 1000))

        @lru_cache(maxsize=None)
        def solve(i, prev_taken, prev_color):
            if i == n:
                return 0
            best = solve(i + 1, False, -1)
            if not (prev_taken and prev_color == colors[i]):
                best = max(best, nums[i] + solve(i + 1, True, colors[i]))
            return best

        result = solve(0, False, -1)
        solve.cache_clear()
        sys.setrecursionlimit(old_limit)
        return result
